title: The Anger You Owe Your Mother
dek: You carry her unexpressed rage in your jaw and your psoas. It is time to stop calling it anxiety and start recognizing it as the biological inheritance of a woman who was never allowed to bare her teeth. This is how you give it back to the earth.
bodyMarkdown:
# The Anger You Owe Your Mother

It’s 4:17 a.m., and the house is still. Outside, the sky leans heavy and gray, the air thick with the damp smell of last night’s rain soaking into the cracked sidewalk. Your body is awake before your mind, a low simmer of tension lodged deep in the right masseter muscle—the one that clenches when you hear her voice in your head. You’ve long been told that anger is ugly, especially the kind of anger that rises from your mother. But what if that anger is not a failing, not a moral lapse you’ve inherited like a curse, but a living, breathing message etched into your flesh and fascia? What if the clenching jaw, the tight psoas, the restless nights scented with the ghost of her perfume, are your body’s way of telling you something you’ve been told to ignore?

This is not pathology. This is biology. This is the anger you owe your mother.

## The Jaw Remembers

The masseter muscle, that thick band running from your cheekbone to your lower jaw, is one of the most telling markers of inherited tension. It’s a muscle that tightens when you’re bracing for impact, holding in words unsaid, and holding back fury deemed unacceptable. When your mother’s anger was volatile or withheld, your jaw learned to hold the story, long after her voice faded from the room. You don’t consciously choose to clench it; the body remembers before the mind catches up.

Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, emphasizes that trauma is stored in the body, not the narrative. In *The Body Keeps the Score*, he writes about how the muscles, the fascia, the very tissues of our bodies hold on to the energetic imprint of past experiences. Your mother’s anger, especially if it was unpredictable, shaming, or silent, is a kind of trauma you carry. It’s not just a memory but a physical sensation, often manifesting as a clenched jaw at odd hours—like the early morning when the world is quiet enough for those old tensions to rise. Sometimes it’s a dull ache, other times a sharp, grinding pain that wakes you from sleep, reminding you of the fury you were taught to bury.

This is not about blaming your mother or yourself. It’s about recognizing that your body is speaking a language older than words. The tightness in your jaw is a somatic echo of her anger, a muscle memory passed down through years of silenced rage and unspoken boundaries.

## The Psoas: The Muscle of Survival and Legacy

If the masseter holds your mother’s anger in your face, the psoas holds it in your core. Nestled deep inside your abdomen, this thick, powerful muscle connects your spine to your legs. It’s often called the muscle of survival, the primal responder that tightens when you feel threatened or unsafe.

When your mother’s anger was a storm you could neither predict nor control, your psoas may have contracted in response, holding your body in a permanent state of readiness. This tension is not simple stiffness; it is the body’s way of preparing you to move, to flee, or to freeze. Karla McLaren, a gifted teacher on the emotional body, describes the psoas as the seat of emotional memory, where anxiety, fear, and yes—anger—can be lodged so deeply that they become part of your baseline physical state.

You might notice this tension as a dull ache in your lower back when you sit too long, or a restless discomfort that wakes you in the middle of the night, exactly at 3:33 a.m., when the world feels most vulnerable. The psoas doesn’t just hold tension—it holds history. Your mother’s anger, the way she carried her own unresolved fury, the way she may have used anger to protect herself or punish others, is encoded in this muscle. It tightens not only in response to your present circumstances but as a legacy of what came before you.

This is why traditional herbal medicine, which I practice, often addresses the psoas through both movement and plant allies. Herbs like [Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20) and [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) have been used for centuries to soothe the nervous system and relax deep muscular tension. They work not by erasing the anger, but by giving your body permission to soften, to release what is no longer needed.

## The Smell of Her Fury

Anger is not just a feeling; it is a smell, a taste, a texture. Sometimes, when you pass a certain perfume in a crowded street or catch a whiff of a particular soap, you are transported back to a time when her anger filled the room like a thick fog. It’s the sharpness of lemon and lavender mixed with something metallic, the smell of a kitchen cleaned too quickly after a fight, or the faint trace of cigarettes burned into the fabric of your childhood home.

Gabor Maté speaks eloquently about the way early emotional environments imprint on the brain and body, shaping not only our mental health but our sensory experiences. He reminds us that the body does not lie; it archives trauma in ways the mind cannot always access. The smell of anger—the scent of tension in the air—becomes a trigger not because you are weak or broken, but because your body is wired to remember what it needs to survive.

You might find yourself waking suddenly in the middle of the night, the air around you thick with the scent of that old anger, even though your mother isn’t there. This is the body’s way of calling you to attention, to acknowledge the debt of anger you owe, not as a punishment, but as a vital inheritance. It is in this acknowledgment that healing begins.

The anger you owe your mother is not a burden to carry silently. It is a living message in your muscles, your breath, your skin. It demands recognition because it is part of your survival, your legacy, and your liberation. To ignore it is to deny a vital part of yourself. To listen—to unclench your jaw, soften your psoas, breathe through the memories—is to reclaim power that has long been held in silence.

## The Diaphragm’s Silent Testament

You might not notice it at first, but the diaphragm is the unwritten ledger of your mother’s anger and your inherited silence. This dome-shaped muscle, nestled just beneath the lungs, contracts and releases with each breath, carrying the weight of unspoken words and held-back fury. When you were small, your breath tightened in response to the sharpness of your mother’s anger — a sudden snap of the masseter muscle in her jaw, the quick rise of her levator scapulae when she turned away. You learned to breathe shallowly, the upper chest puffing out in a guarded shield, while the diaphragm below stiffened, locked into a locked-down state.

Resmaa Menakem, a somatic therapist who names the trauma in the body’s soft tissues, teaches that trauma lodges not just in the brain but in the fascia — the connective tissue that wraps every muscle, every organ, every cell in a continuous web. When your mother’s anger was too dangerous to face, your fascia tightened around your diaphragm and ribs, creating a physical barrier to your full breath, your full self. This isn’t metaphor. When the fascia constricts, it literally limits the movement of the diaphragm, trapping breath and emotion together. You can smell the faint musk of stale sweat and stress in the skin over the ribs at night, your breath shallow and fast, the diaphragm barely flexing.

This muscle remembers her fury as a threat. It contracts before you even realize you’re holding your breath. This contraction sends a cascade of messages through your vagus nerve, signaling danger and shutting down your parasympathetic nervous system. You become a body that’s always on alert, a body that’s learned to swallow rage and spit silence to survive.

## The Herbal Allies in the Battle Within

You are not without allies. The plants have your back, holding the same reverence for the body’s wisdom that teachers like Karla McLaren do for emotional fluency. McLaren, who has spent decades decoding anger as a messenger rather than a malfunction, reminds us that fury is a language. When your diaphragm is tight and your breath shallow, herbs can serve as translators, helping your body soften the message so you can hear it without fear.

Consider the ancient wisdom of motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca). This herb is named for its capacity to soothe the heart and calm the diaphragm, loosening the grip of tension that feels like a physical knot just beneath your sternum. Motherwort works its magic on the fascia too, easing the binding and inviting a more fluid movement of breath and blood. It’s not a sedative that dulls your edge — rather, it’s a medicine that restores the body's natural rhythm to rage and rest alike.

Another ally is skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora), a nervine herb that helps quiet the overactive nervous system without shutting down your alertness. When your body is trapped in the fight-or-flight response, skullcap encourages the diaphragm to descend fully, releasing the pressure that builds in the trapezius and levator scapulae muscles of your neck and shoulders. I suggest steeping these herbs into a tea in the early evening, after the sun has dropped behind the trees and the air carries the sharp bite of approaching night. The warmth and aroma open the lungs, invite a long exhale — and with it, a little more space for your anger to be felt and understood.

## A Somatic Practice to Free the Breath and Face the Fury

Now, let’s bring this into your body. Stand barefoot on the cool floor, feeling the textures of your skin against the fabric of your clothes. Place your hands on the lower ribs, just where the diaphragm hides beneath the surface. Take three slow, deliberate breaths, inhaling through your nose and exhaling through your mouth with a soft sigh. Notice how your ribs expand sideways, not just forward. This lateral expansion is the diaphragm’s dance — the body remembering its capacity to hold and release.

On your fourth breath, inhale deeply, feeling the tension in your diaphragm and fascia. Hold the breath for just a moment, enough to acknowledge the tightness, the constriction that has long been your silent companion. Now, exhale fully, letting your ribs collapse inward, your diaphragm release the stored tension. As you exhale, imagine the fascia around your ribs and diaphragm loosening like the gentle unraveling of a tightly wound thread.

Repeat this cycle of breath five times, each time inviting a little more softness around the diaphragm, a little more spaciousness in the muscles that once stiffened in fear of your mother’s anger. This practice is not about controlling your breath but about witnessing the way your body remembers and resists. You are learning to meet your fury where it lives — in the hard-fought places of your body — and honor it as your own.

The body is a fierce archivist. The diaphragm holds your history, the herbs offer their healing dialogue, and your breath becomes the language of reclamation. This is not about forgetting or excusing the past. It is about retrieving your own voice from the rubble of silenced rage. The anger you owe your mother is also a debt to yourself — a call to breathe fully, fiercely, and without shame.

Part 3: The Anger You Owe Your Mother — Resolution in the Body

You wake before dawn, the sky still a bruised violet, the air sharp with the scent of night jasmine blooming in the garden outside your window. Your neck is stiff, that familiar tightness gripping the levator scapulae—the muscle that climbs like a narrow ladder from the top of your shoulder blade to the side of your neck. It’s the muscle that remembers every time you swallowed your fury, every invisible nod you gave to the unspoken family rules that told you your anger was a betrayal, an ugliness. You cradle the back of your neck, your fingers tracing the taut band of muscle that feels like a coil of wire beneath your skin. This is where your anger lives, folded into your fascia, refusing to let go until you acknowledge it.

The levator scapulae is notorious for harboring the weight of emotional burdens. It tightens when you hold your head low, when you carry the invisible load of shame or unexpressed frustration. When your mother’s voice echoes in your mind—sharp, disappointed, or silent—your body reacts before your thoughts can catch up. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your body remembers the unspoken message: “Don’t make waves. Don’t be too much.” The trapezius, the psoas, even your diaphragm—all these muscles conspire in a somatic symphony of restraint and repression. The question is: how do you begin to untangle this contracted muscle memory without betraying the parts of you that still cling to safety in silence?

You start by grounding yourself in the present moment, the only place where your body can begin to rewrite its story. The ground beneath your feet is cool, the texture of rough concrete or soft earth pressing into the soles, anchoring you. You inhale through your nose, the air filling your lungs like a slow, deliberate wave, and exhale through your mouth with a soft sigh that releases some of the grip in your neck. This breath isn’t about forcing peace or quieting your anger—it’s about making space for it. The diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your ribs, expands and contracts like a bellows, moving the anger from something stuck in your throat or jaw into a rhythm that can be felt and recognized.

In the quiet, you feel the subtle pull along the levator scapulae as your head naturally lifts and rotates, small movements that mimic the gentle unwinding of a spring. You’re not trying to fix the muscle as much as you are listening to what it’s been trying to tell you. The jaw relaxes, the masseter unwinding from its clenched state, and with that release, the body’s message becomes clearer: your anger was never ugly. It was a signal, a vital message you owed your mother—and ultimately yourself. This acknowledgment is a radical act of self-honoring, a reclaiming of a power long denied.

Herbs become your allies here, not as sedatives to dull your feelings but as medicines to support your nervous system’s capacity to hold discomfort. You steep a cup of [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20) in hot water, its earthy, slightly bitter scent filling your kitchen as the steam rises in gentle spirals. Skullcap doesn’t erase your anger; it softens the edges of your nervous system’s alarm bells, making room for you to sit with your fury without tumbling into overwhelm. You might follow this with a tincture of [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20), an adaptogen that helps your body recalibrate stress responses, particularly in the muscles where your tension accumulates.

Somatic teachers like Resmaa Menakem remind us that trauma and emotion live in the body’s tissues long after the original event has passed. Your levator scapulae is a repository for this intergenerational transmission, a physical ledger of what was never spoken aloud but deeply felt. When you ground, breathe, and soften into these sensations, you participate in a quiet revolution: disrupting the cycle of silence and shame. This is not about confronting your mother with accusations or rewriting your childhood narrative; it’s about reclaiming the parts of your body and self that were made small by an unacknowledged fury.

You might find yourself drawn to movement practices that honor this slow unwinding—gentle neck rotations in the early morning light, the deliberate shrug of shoulders, the slow roll of the head from side to side. These movements are not about perfection or performance; they are invitations to the levator scapulae to relax, to release, to remember that it is safe to hold space for anger without fear. The body’s fascia, that connective tissue web, responds to these invitations with a subtle softening, like the first thaw of a long winter.

Every night, when the world is cloaked in darkness and the only light comes from a single candle flickering on your bedside table, you practice grounding with your feet firmly on the floor and your hands resting gently on your thighs. The world outside is silent except for the occasional rustle of leaves or the distant call of a night bird. In these moments, the anger you owe your mother—and yourself—does not need words. It needs presence. It needs the slow, steady pulse of your breath, the gentle release of your neck muscles, the quiet acceptance of your body’s wisdom.

The anger you owe your mother is not a debt you pay with resentment or bitterness. It is a gift you give yourself—a reclamation of your body’s right to express its truths. It lives in your levator scapulae, your diaphragm, your trapezius, waiting for you to meet it not with judgment but with curiosity and care. This is how the body heals: not by forgetting, but by remembering differently.

---

**Q:** Why is the levator scapulae such a common site for holding anger and tension?

**A:** The levator scapulae connects your shoulder blade to your neck and is intimately involved in movements like shrugging and head rotation. Because it supports the head and shoulders, it is highly sensitive to emotional states that involve vigilance or contraction, such as suppressed anger or stress. When anger is not expressed, this muscle tightens as part of a protective pattern, holding your body in a state of readiness or restraint.

**Q:** How does grounding help in resolving anger held in the body?

**A:** Grounding helps you reconnect with the present moment and your physical body, providing a stable base from which to feel difficult sensations safely. When anger is held in muscles like the levator scapulae, grounding practices—such as feeling your feet on the floor or focusing on your breath—can reduce the nervous system’s alarm response, allowing the muscles to soften and the anger to be acknowledged rather than ignored or suppressed.

**Q:** What role do herbs play in working with the somatic experience of anger?

**A:** Herbs like skullcap and ashwagandha support the nervous system by modulating stress responses and calming overactive alarm systems, without dulling your emotional experience. They create a physiological environment where you can sit with anger and tension in your body without being overwhelmed, facilitating deeper awareness and release.

**Q:** Is expressing anger verbally necessary for healing these bodily tensions?

**A:** Not necessarily. Healing the body’s tension patterns related to anger can begin with somatic awareness and movement. While verbal expression can be helpful for some, the body’s wisdom often precedes words. Allowing the muscles to soften and the fascia to release through mindful movement and breath can initiate healing even without immediate verbal articulation.

**Q:** How can I continue this work if my mother is still in my life and our relationship is complicated?

**A:** The work of reclaiming your anger is about your relationship with yourself first and foremost, not necessarily about changing your mother or your dynamic with her. By attending to your body’s messages and creating space for your anger, you build internal resources and boundaries that can help you navigate the relationship with greater clarity and strength.
